Methodology White Paper
Version 1.1

Marketâ€‘Implied Inflation Expectations

A reproducible signal that summarises forward inflation expectations inferred from TIPS breakevens. Built to complement the Fed Inflation signal by focusing on market pricing rather than realised CPI.

Why: CPI shows history; TIPS breakevens reveal forward expectations. Sources: FRED (T5YIFR, T10YIE).

Abstract

We construct a monthly signal using FREDâ€™s T5YIFR (5â€‘Year, 5â€‘Year Forward Inflation Expectation Rate) and T10YIE (10â€‘Year Breakeven Inflation Rate). Each is transformed to YoY and 3â€‘month annualised changes, standardised via robust rolling zâ€‘scores, and combined into a Marketâ€‘Implied Inflation Expectations (MIIE) composite. Regimes map to RISING, STABLE, or FALLING expectations. A comparison hook aligns MIIE with the Fed Inflation and CPI vs PPI signals.

1. Data (FRED Identifiers)

  • T5YIFR â€” 5â€‘Year, 5â€‘Year Forward Inflation Expectation Rate (derived from 5â€‘ and 10â€‘year breakevens, % p.a.)
  • T10YIE â€” 10â€‘Year Breakeven Inflation Rate (nominal 10y minus TIPS 10y, % p.a.)

Both series are resampled to monthâ€‘end with bounded forwardâ€‘fill (â‰¤2 months) to accommodate publication lags. Titles and lastâ€‘observation dates are captured for documentation.

What these series represent:

  • Breakeven inflation is the difference between a nominal Treasury constantâ€‘maturity yield and a TIPS (inflationâ€‘indexed) constantâ€‘maturity yield of the same tenor. It is often interpreted as inflation compensation, not â€œpureâ€ expected inflation.
  • T10YIE is derived from the 10â€‘year nominal constantâ€‘maturity yield (DGS10) and the 10â€‘year TIPS constantâ€‘maturity yield (DFII10) as published on FRED.
  • T5YIFR is a forward rate: expected average inflation over the fiveâ€‘year period starting five years ahead (a 5y5y forward). FRED constructs it mechanically from 5â€‘ and 10â€‘year breakevens, so it inherits the same market microstructure effects as underlying breakevens.
  • Units & frequency: Both are percent per annum, available at daily frequency on FRED; this signal samples monthâ€‘end values to align with other macro composites.

Data source: St. Louis Fed FRED, which republishes the underlying Treasury constantâ€‘maturity estimates and derived breakeven/forward series.

2. Data Quality, Cleaning & Validation

  • Recent availability check: log last observation date; flag stale (>62 days) inputs.
  • Bounded forwardâ€‘fill: up to 2 months max; else mark as missing_critical.
  • Minimum history: require â‰¥120 months coverage for robust transforms; otherwise adapt window (â‰¥60 months) and emit a warning.
  • Outlier guard: winsorise 0.5â€“99.5th percentiles before zâ€‘scoring (configurable).
  • Breakeven distortions: breakevens embed (i) expected inflation, (ii) an inflation risk premium, and (iii) a TIPS liquidity premium; QE and â€œsafeâ€‘assetâ€ demand can move nominal and real yields unevenly, shifting breakevens without a oneâ€‘forâ€‘one change in expectations.
  • Liquidity overlays (recommended): record a liquidity_regime and optionally downâ€‘weight level components when liquidity is stressed. Practical proxies include TIPS trading liquidity (bidâ€‘ask/volume), nominalâ€“TIPS fitting errors, or publicly available liquidity indices/ETFs when direct microstructure data are unavailable.

A data_quality_flag field records staleness, ffill counts, and window adaptations.

3. Component Transforms

Given monthly series x_t in pct points:

YoY(x)_t = x_t - x_{t-12}
Ann\;3m(x)_t = 4 \times (x_t - x_{t-3})
Level(x)_t = x_t

Constructed components: t5yie_level, t5yie_yoy, t5yie_ann3m, t10yie_level, t10yie_yoy, t10yie_ann3m.

4. Standardisation (Rolling zâ€‘scores)

Each component is scaled using a rolling window W=120 months. Robust zâ€‘scores use median/MAD; otherwise mean/std.

z_t = \dfrac{x_t - \text{median}_{W}(x)}{1.4826\,\text{MAD}_{W}(x)} \quad \text{(robust)}

If insufficient history, use adaptive window (â‰¥60m) and emit a warning.

5. Weighting & Composite Construction

Let z^k_t be the zâ€‘score for component k. Default weights emphasise levels (forward expectation) with supporting momentum (YoY, 3m ann.). Missing inputs are reâ€‘normalised out.

{
  "t5yie_level_z": 0.35, "t5yie_yoy_z": 0.15, "t5yie_ann3m_z": 0.10,
  "t10yie_level_z": 0.30, "t10yie_yoy_z": 0.07, "t10yie_ann3m_z": 0.03
}
MIIE_t = \sum_k w_k\, z^k_t,\quad \sum_k w_k = 1

6. Scaling & Regime Mapping

We provide a bounded score (0â€“100) and categorical regimes for communication:

Score_t = 100\times\dfrac{MIIE_t - \min_W MIIE}{\max_W MIIE - \min_W MIIE}
  • RISING: z(MIIE)t â‰¥ 1.0 (expectations moving higher)
  • STABLE: âˆ’0.5 < z(MIIE)t < 1.0
  • FALLING: z(MIIE)t â‰¤ âˆ’0.5 (expectations moving lower)

7. Comparison Hooks

  • Fed Inflation Signal: Compare MIIE regime to composite inflation pressure; divergence suggests markets pricing a turn ahead of CPI.
  • CPI vs PPI: If PPI < CPI while MIIE is FALLING, it strengthens a disinflation narrative; the converse signals pressure.

These hooks require only the other signalsâ€™ latest regime/score; no structural coupling is assumed.

8. Implementation Notes (Python)

# Fetch (fredapi) â†’ monthly end
t5 = fred.get_series("T5YIE")
t10 = fred.get_series("T10YIE")

# Resample to month-end & bounded ffill (<=2)
t5m = t5.resample("M").last().ffill(limit=2)
t10m = t10.resample("M").last().ffill(limit=2)

# Transforms (levels, YoY, 3m ann. in pct points)
def ann_3m(s): return 4*(s - s.shift(3))
components = {
  "t5yie_level": t5m, "t5yie_yoy": t5m - t5m.shift(12), "t5yie_ann3m": ann_3m(t5m),
  "t10yie_level": t10m, "t10yie_yoy": t10m - t10m.shift(12), "t10yie_ann3m": ann_3m(t10m),
}

# Robust rolling z-scores (W=120, fallback >=60)
# Combine with weights â†’ MIIE, then scale to 0â€“100 and map regime.

9. Reproducibility, Audit & Monitoring

  • Persist FRED IDs, retrieval timestamps, resampling policy, and zâ€‘score window.
  • Log cleaning (ffill count), staleness flags, and window adaptations.
  • Store panels: raw, transforms, zâ€‘scores, contributions, composite, regimes.

10. Interpretation Guide (How to Read the Signal)

Key identity: breakeven inflation is best treated as inflation compensation:

Breakeven â‰ˆ Expected Inflation + Inflation Risk Premium âˆ’ TIPS Liquidity Premium

This means the signal can move because markets update inflation expectations, but also because risk premia or liquidity conditions change.

  • RISING regime: inflation compensation is increasing relative to its longâ€‘run distribution (z(MIIE) â‰¥ 1.0). Typical macro associations: higher inflation pricing, higher probability of restrictive policy, and (often) upward pressure on real yieldsâ€”unless the move is dominated by liquidity/premia effects.
  • FALLING regime: inflation compensation is declining (z(MIIE) â‰¤ âˆ’0.5). Typical associations: disinflation pricing and/or flightâ€‘toâ€‘quality dynamics that compress inflation compensation.
  • STABLE regime: midâ€‘range zâ€‘scores suggest expectations are not moving decisively relative to history; focus shifts to crossâ€‘signal confirmation (CPI/PPI, growth, liquidity, credit).

10.1 Practical checks (avoid common misreads)

  • Liquidity stress: during riskâ€‘off episodes, TIPS liquidity can deteriorate and mechanically depress breakevens. If a liquidity proxy is flagged â€œstressedâ€, interpret a FALLING reading with caution (it may be liquidityâ€‘driven rather than a clean shift in expectations).
  • QE / supply effects: largeâ€‘scale asset purchases and shifts in the supply of safe duration can change term premia and relative pricing across nominal vs real curves; treat level moves as less informative when policy interventions are dominant.
  • Use zâ€‘scores as â€œdistance from normalâ€: robust longâ€‘window zâ€‘scores are intended to reduce sensitivity to transient dislocations. Extremes (e.g., |z| â‰¥ 2) are better viewed as â€œunusual pricingâ€ than as point forecasts of inflation.
  • Crossâ€‘validation: compare MIIE to realised inflation signals (Fed Inflation; CPI vs PPI) and to realâ€‘yield/termâ€‘premium diagnostics. Persistent divergence is often more informative than singleâ€‘month flips.

10.2 Literature anchor (selected)

  • Fed Board and academic work on extracting inflation compensation from TIPS yield curves and the role of risk premia (GÃ¼rkaynak, Sack & Wright).
  • Evidence that ignoring TIPS liquidity can mislead inference about expectations and inflation risk premia (Dâ€™Amico, Kim & Wei; related centralâ€‘bank summaries).
  • Documentation of persistent TIPSâ€“Treasury mispricing consistent with liquidity/supply effects (Fleckenstein, Longstaff & Lustig).
  • Evidence that QE and safeâ€‘asset demand shift term premia and relative pricing across rates markets (Krishnamurthy & Vissingâ€‘Jorgensen).

12. Governance & Change Control

  • Quarterly review of components, thresholds, weights, and backtests.
  • Document rationale for changes; maintain semantic versioning.
Â© 2026. Printâ€‘friendly specification for the Marketâ€‘Implied Inflation Expectations signal.
References (links):
FRED: T10YIE; T5YIFR. â€¢ GÃ¼rkaynak, Sack & Wright (2010), AEJ: Macroeconomics. â€¢ Dâ€™Amico, Kim & Wei (Fed/FEDS). â€¢ Fleckenstein, Longstaff & Lustig (2014), Journal of Finance. â€¢ Krishnamurthy & Vissingâ€‘Jorgensen (2011), Brookings Papers.
